Nototo lets you build a virt…

broke: relying on opaque Twitter algorithm to find new people to follow

bespoke: directly perusing follower graph of interesting people to find new people to follow

Basic methodology: grab followers with Twitter API, import into Gephi, filter out accounts with >100k followers, compute "Modularity", color code based on modularity, size nodes based on in-degree, layout with ForceAtlas2.

It's fun to play "identify the communities". Any help in the ones I can't identify or mess up are appreciated. Suggestions for accounts to do next are welcome. PM me if you want any of the Gephi files.

Several people asked me for a way to interactively explore visualizations of Twitter networks (especially with search). Here it is: https://t.co/ckxuhNnf02

Features:
- 2D and 3D
- Search
- Zoom, pan, etc
- Includes names and bios
- Multilevel communities
- Mobile (2D) https://t.co/foBeeEjDDd
When you click a search result it will take you to the node. (In grouped mode, it will also scroll to the account's location in the list of accounts in the supernode) https://t.co/rsXaG6xtOk

Here's what multilevel communities look like. The graph gets smaller and easier to read at higher levels. Notice that the colors are consistent between levels. (The orientation isn't, currently. I.e., it might be rotated or flipped between the levels.) https://t.co/9gkWDkfb6N

Accounts are put into communities which are then merged into supernodes (one supernode per community). The process then repeats at higher levels: communities are found among the supernodes, then all the supernodes of a community merge into a supersupernode.
More here on grouped mode and multilevel communities https://t.co/TzsTE74hGp  and https://t.co/VOovJZJesv Here's an example of a list of accounts in a supernode https://t.co/6SBCSuoJUT

Here's what 3D looks like. You can fly around with keyboard controls. (No 3D on mobile yet, sorry). Everything works similarly to 2D mode. https://t.co/HhvdrUPwF4
Mobile has zooming and panning, but it currently only supports the 2D version of the first grouped level, i.e. "2D 1G". If I figure out a clean way to switch levels from mobile I'll add that.

Despite preliminary filtering, the graphs I post often have a couple of totally random/irrelevant clustering. Let's see why... Currently, the filter I have in place for this is an in-degree filter. E.g., at least 10 people in the network need to follow this person.

1/n https://t.co/pWNGa3eNUB

Say, e.g., that all the seed accounts for the graph are software devs. Let's say seed A is really into standup comedy (or w/e) and follows 20 standup accounts, all of which follow each other. Then, each standup account has at least 20 in-degree, so they don't get filtered out.

How can we fix this? My first thought was to just filter every account which isn't followed by at least 2 seed accounts. But, testing this out it seems too extreme (see histograms). If I did this then most accounts would get filtered out. What's a way to weaken this filter? https://t.co/VW3KtQ8L2r

Here's my idea: Every node is already followed by at least one seed account. Call that Seed A. Let's require that for every node x, there needs to be a path of length <=2 from a seed account to node x and which doesn't include Seed A. What does this mean?

It means that EITHER 1) node x is followed by at least 2 seed accounts, OR 2) one of the follows of one of the seeds who aren't Seed A follow node x. Note we exclude the case where Seed B follows Seed A who (we already know) follows node x, for obvious reasons.

Anyway, this is the cleanest idea I can think of currently. It's a reasonably weak condition. It could still happen, of course, that Seed B (or one of the people they follow) is also really into standup comedy. Then it's doomed. But, it's better, I think.

Note, you can extend the filter like: there needs to be at least two paths, three paths, etc. 

Anyone know a better idea?

Most graph layout algorithms, like my preferred algorithm ForceAtlas2, are called force-directed layouts. They treat the graph like a physical system and use its equilibrium as the resulting layout.

In the field of "network geometry" there is a totally different approach. The observed edges in the graph are considered to be generated from an underlying process which corresponds to a (hyperbolic) geometry.

The idea is to try to infer that underlying geometry.

I thought it would be fun to try out this hyperbolic layout inference on the rats graph. If you want to see the result now, here it is 

Full res (too big for Twitter): 
https://t.co/YlOmQ0TNvg https://t.co/sTDxPBXOdV
It should be noted that the above isn't the *exact* layout. I sized the nodes and jiggled the layout a bit to remove overlap so the labels could be read.

Visualizing relationships between senators in the 116th US Congress (current) based on cosponsorship of bills.

Democrats https://t.co/LgMFZATy41
Republicans https://t.co/Popq6nRKGe
Center? https://t.co/7DF3Ibc2GY

Technical details:

The original graph is bipartite with one side being senators and the other side being bills. Edges are (co)sponsorship.

The colors are inferred with the bipartite stochastic block model (BiSBM) from @oneofyen and @DanLarremore

The nodes are sized using the BiRank algorithm from @yang3kc

I used the bipartite configuration model from @__Sarawalk__ to do the projection onto the one mode (of senators).

So, I did the sizing and the community detection on the full bipartite network, and then used that information to make the bipartite projection readable.

The network without projection is far less readable because it includes all 100 senators plus over 4k bills https://t.co/iVLaKGwK3Y

Oh, and the weight of the edges is coming from the 'surprise' of the number of shared cosponsorships (relative to the null model). 'Surprise' being the negative log of the probability of that many cosponsorships occurring in the null model

@deepfates typical network centrality measures basically generalize this intuition: some follows 'count' more than others. but, instead of only looking at first order it is recursive. everyone gets points for how many points their followers have. 

simplest is eigenvector centrality https://t.co/CM4yiuoSe3

@deepfates further improvements to the basic scheme are in Katz Centrality and PageRank.

I show PageRank on Twitterverse (with the obvious caveat that it's computed only with respect to the specific subgraph, not all of Twitter, ofc)

@deepfates @architectonyx personally haven't come across an established metric for this, but on Twitterverse I just made one up which I called niche factor: PageRank / (total number of followers).

Kinda jank in some cases (only couple of followers), but still interesting

@deepfates re PR question: Katz centrality gives everyone 'free' points such that if you're followed by 1000 accounts each with 0 followers it still counts for something. PageRank takes another step and penalizes points based on how many followers the person has
@deepfates i.e., it formalizes what people call "rare follows". Being followed by a 100k followers account 'matters' more if they follow only 100 people, vs 1000.

@Ethan_smith_20 @aman_gif noting that you don't need to use complex numbers to use this. it has an elegant interpretation even with real numbers.

Let x = [-1, 2]

softmax(x) ≈ [0.047, 0.953]
dipole_softmax(x, offset=0) ≈ [0.269, 0.731]
phase_preserving_softmax(x) ≈ [-0.269,  0.731]

@Ethan_smith_20 @aman_gif i.e., it gives the same results as your dipole softmax but it carries the sign through. this makes intuitive sense: when we take the weighted mean a token with a negative dot product should have a negative weight
